concept: Specification / Explicit Set of Technical Requirements
description: An explicit set of required properties, dimensions, interfaces, conditions, or performance criteria for a component, system, material, process, or work result. A specification may be incomplete, mistaken, contested, or issued without legitimate authority. The word does not mean a standard, law, design, method, instruction, exact source text, proof of compliance, or permission for use.
sound_symbolism: Precise 'kiro' opens the required details, while complete 'sholu' gathers them into an inspectable set. The word joins exactness with wholeness without claiming that either has actually been achieved.
grammatical_notes: A countable noun: 'ha kirosholu ha henoraki thelima.' (This specification describes the capacity.) Carry exact values, units, identifiers, versions, standards, and quoted requirements in 'patha ... patho'. A system satisfies a specification with ordinary 'heno'; this does not itself certify compliance. Distinct from 'kiremoa' (criterion), 'norae' (method), and 'kirothe' (define).

pillars.solarpunk-values: Open specifications can make shared infrastructure understandable and repairable while leaving authorship, authority, access, and consequences open to challenge.
pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: Separating a written requirement from authority and compliance prevents technical language from turning an instruction into unquestionable command.

tags.cognition: a reference against which properties and performance may be assessed
tags.creation: an explicit set of requirements for design, work, or technical provision
tags.dialogue: a shareable statement of requirements that may still be disputed or revised
